title: MacArthur Fellowship ("Genius Grant")
description: An unrestricted $800,000 award paid over five years to individuals across any field who show exceptional creativity and promise for significant future contributions. There is no application process; recipients are identified through confidential nominations and a review by an anonymous selection committee.
org: John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation
eligibility: Must be a U.S. citizen or resident; cannot hold elective office or high-level government positions; no age or field restrictions; cannot self-apply, must be nominated through the Foundation's confidential nomination process.
prepResources: Review past Fellows' profiles on macfound.org to understand the creativity/impact bar; since it is nomination-only, building a public track record of original work (publications, media coverage, exhibitions) is the only real preparation.

title: Guggenheim Fellowship
description: A prestigious fellowship supporting individuals who have already demonstrated exceptional capacity for productive scholarship or creative ability in the arts, giving them time and support to pursue a significant project. Fellows are selected annually across the U.S. and Canada through a rigorous adviser-based review process.
eligibility: Citizens or permanent residents of the United States or Canada; mid-career professionals with a demonstrated record in their field; not currently enrolled in secondary, undergraduate, graduate, or postgraduate programs; prior Fellows are ineligible to reapply.
prepResources: Read the Application Guidelines and FAQ on gf.org before writing your project proposal; have strong reference letters lined up from established figures in your field since the Foundation relies on confidential advisers.

title: MacDowell Fellowship (Artist Residency)
description: A fully-funded residency of up to eight weeks at MacDowell's campus in Peterborough, NH, giving artists free studio space, private accommodations, and meals to focus on their work. Need-based financial assistance is also available to cover travel, lost income, and childcare costs.
eligibility: Open to artists in architecture, film/video, interdisciplinary arts, literature, music composition, theatre, and visual arts; must be at least 21 and not currently enrolled in a degree-seeking program; may apply only once every 24 months.
prepResources: Prepare work samples from the last four years per the online portal requirements; MacDowell recently dropped the reference-letter requirement, so the work sample and project statement carry the most weight.

title: Yaddo Artist Residency
description: A residency at Yaddo's estate in Saratoga Springs, NY, providing a private bedroom, private studio, and full room and board so artists can focus solely on their creative work. Residencies run from two weeks up to two months depending on the season.
eligibility: Generative artists in literature, visual art, film & video, music composition, or performance art who work at a professional level or show strong professional promise; not enrolled in a degree program; cannot have applied within the previous two calendar years.
prepResources: Submit your strongest, most recent work samples through the SlideRoom portal; note the $35 nonrefundable application fee and the July 1 (summer cycle) or December 20 (winter cycle) deadlines.

title: Creative Capital Award
description: An open-call national grant program giving individual artists unrestricted project grants of up to $50,000 plus professional development support to create innovative new work. It is one of the only major U.S. grant programs that funds artists directly across all disciplines through a public application rather than nomination.
eligibility: U.S. citizens, permanent residents, Tribal ID holders, or O-1 visa holders; at least 25 years old with a minimum of 5 years of professional artistic practice; cannot be enrolled in a degree-granting program.
prepResources: Download the official Creative Capital Application Handbook PDF from their site before starting; the same application is automatically considered for the companion State of the Art Prize ($10,000 per state).

title: NASA International Space Apps Challenge
description: The world's largest annual space-themed hackathon, where participants of any age or background form teams over a single weekend to build solutions to real challenges posed by NASA and space agency partners. It's free to join, fully open to the public, and run simultaneously at local host events worldwide and virtually.
eligibility: Open to all ages, skill levels, and professional backgrounds; no coding experience required; participants can join solo or as part of a team, in-person at a local event or virtually.
location: Global (in-person local events and virtual participation)
prepResources: Register early once sign-ups open at spaceappschallenge.org and browse past years' challenge statements to understand the format; no technical background is required, so teams often recruit designers and storytellers alongside coders.

title: ARC Prize 2026 (Kaggle)
description: A $2 million global AI research competition hosted on Kaggle challenging participants to build systems that solve novel abstract reasoning puzzles (ARC-AGI benchmarks), aimed at accelerating progress toward general AI. It includes multiple tracks with milestone and final cash prizes.
org: ARC Prize Inc., hosted on Kaggle
eligibility: Open globally to individuals or teams of 1-5 (students, professionals, and hobbyists all eligible); winning solutions must be open-sourced under a permissive license; no internet access allowed during evaluation, which excludes API-based LLM solutions.
prepResources: Read the official ARC Prize technical guide and past winning solution write-ups at arcprize.org before starting; join via the linked Kaggle competition pages to access starter notebooks and the leaderboard.

title: Future Vision XPRIZE
description: A $3.5M+ global competition (presented with Google and XPRIZE) challenging creators anywhere to produce a short sci-fi film/trailer depicting a hopeful, technology-enabled vision of humanity's future. Winners receive cash prizes and, for the grand prize, an equity investment to help develop the concept further.
eligibility: Open to creators anywhere in the world; submissions may use any production method including AI tools, traditional filming, or animation; requires a maximum 3-minute trailer plus a written treatment and synopsis.
location: Global (submissions online; finals event in Los Angeles)
prepResources: Review the official rules and submission template at futurevisionxprize.com before producing your trailer; focus on a hopeful, concrete vision of the future rather than dystopian themes, which the brief explicitly asks for.

title: National Geographic Society Level I Grant
description: Seed funding of up to $20,000 for individuals starting out in science, conservation, storytelling, education, or technology fields to lead a one-year project aligned with National Geographic's focus areas. It's designed as an entry point into the Society's Explorer community for people who haven't received an NGS grant before.
eligibility: Applicants must be 18 or older and serve as the actual project leader; only available to those who have not previously received a National Geographic Society grant; those working outside their home country must include a local collaborator; only one active proposal at a time.
prepResources: Review the How to Apply and Eligibility pages on nationalgeographic.org before starting a Grants Portal account; project start dates must be at least 6 months after the deadline, so plan timelines accordingly.

title: World Bank Robert S. McNamara Fellowship
description: An eight-month, DC-based research fellowship pairing early-career economists with World Bank researchers on development-economics topics, aimed at candidates from developing countries.
eligibility: Enrolled in or recently completed a master's/PhD in economics or a related development field; nationals of World Bank member developing countries.
prepResources: Review the World Bank's research output on development economics and prior fellows' projects before drafting a research proposal.

title: Fulbright-Nehru Master's Fellowship
description: A fully funded fellowship for Indian professionals to pursue a one- to two-year master's degree at a U.S. university, covering tuition, living costs, travel and visa support.
eligibility: Indian citizens with a bachelor's degree and at least three years of relevant professional experience.
prepResources: Check USIEF's eligible fields list and start GRE/TOEFL prep early; strong statements of purpose tie the degree to a return-to-India career plan.

title: Chevening Scholarships
description: A UK government scholarship funding a one-year master's degree at any UK university for emerging global leaders, with an emphasis on future impact back home.
eligibility: At least two years of work experience and an undergraduate degree; must return to home country for two years after the award.
prepResources: Chevening's own guidance on writing the four required essays is the best starting point, and UK university offers must be secured in parallel.

title: Commonwealth Shared Scholarships
description: Full master's scholarships at UK universities for talented students from lower- and middle-income Commonwealth countries who could not otherwise afford to study abroad.
eligibility: Citizens of eligible developing Commonwealth countries; typically applied for via a UK university's shortlist rather than directly.
prepResources: Check which UK universities currently participate in the Shared Scholarships scheme, since applications usually route through them.

title: Schwarzman Scholars Program
description: A fully funded one-year master's program in global affairs at Tsinghua University in Beijing, designed to build a network of future leaders with firsthand understanding of China.
eligibility: Bachelor's degree holders roughly ages 18-28 with demonstrated leadership potential; no prior China or Mandarin experience required.
prepResources: The program's own admissions essays and video-interview prompts are the best gauge of fit; look at past scholars' varied academic backgrounds.

title: Obama Foundation Scholars Program
description: A nine-month, non-degree leadership program at Columbia University for young professionals already working on community and civic challenges in their home countries.
eligibility: Emerging civic leaders with a demonstrated track record of community impact; program has run cohorts based at Columbia and University of Chicago.
prepResources: Highlight concrete, on-the-ground impact work rather than credentials alone — the program selects for practitioners, not just academics.

title: Rotary Peace Fellowship
description: A fully funded fellowship at one of Rotary's Peace Centers worldwide for a master's degree or professional development certificate in peace and conflict resolution.
eligibility: At least three years of relevant work experience in peace, development, or conflict-affected settings; strong proficiency in English.
prepResources: Applications go through local Rotary districts first, so connect with a district Rotary club well ahead of the global deadline.

title: Quad Fellowship
org: Institute of International Education / Quad Governments (Australia, India, Japan, US)
description: A one-time award supporting 100 students annually from Australia, India, Japan and the US to pursue STEM master's or doctoral study in the United States.
eligibility: Citizens of the four Quad nations who have been admitted to or are pursuing a STEM graduate program in the US.
prepResources: Applications open once per year on the Quad Fellowship site; secure a US graduate program admission in parallel where possible.

title: Aga Khan International Scholarship Programme
description: A part-grant, part-loan postgraduate scholarship for outstanding students from select developing countries who have no other means of financing further study.
eligibility: Nationals of eligible countries with an outstanding academic record and demonstrated financial need; must show no alternative funding is available.
prepResources: Financial need documentation is scrutinized closely, so gather bank/income records early alongside the academic case for study.

title: World Trade Organization Young Professionals Programme
description: A structured placement within WTO Secretariat divisions in Geneva that builds practical expertise in international trade law and policy for early-career professionals.
eligibility: Advanced degree in a trade-relevant field (economics, law, international relations); under roughly 32 years old.
prepResources: Trade-policy coursework or a thesis on WTO-related issues strengthens an application; the WTO's own vacancy page lists exact yearly criteria.

title: World Bank Group Young Professionals Program
description: A two-year rotational leadership program that typically leads into longer-term employment at the World Bank, giving high-potential graduates early exposure to global development operations.
eligibility: Advanced degree (master's or PhD) with relevant experience; strong quantitative and development-economics background typically preferred.
prepResources: Read the eligibility criteria on the WBG careers site closely each cycle, since the program only runs annually with a narrow window.

title: World Bank Group Internship Program
description: A paid internship exposing graduate and advanced undergraduate students to the World Bank's development work, typically over a summer or academic term.
eligibility: Enrolled in a full-time graduate program (or final year of undergraduate) in a development-relevant field.
prepResources: The Bank's careers site lists application windows twice a year; tailoring your resume to a specific Global Practice area helps.

title: Asian Development Bank Internship Program
description: A research-oriented internship placing graduate students within ADB departments to contribute to live development projects across Asia and the Pacific.
eligibility: Enrolled in a master's or PhD program in economics, finance, engineering, or a related development field.
prepResources: ADB reviews applications on a rolling basis tied to department needs, so applying with a specific research interest in mind helps.

title: AIIB Graduate Program
description: A two-year rotational program moving new graduates through investment, risk, strategy and operations functions at a major multilateral infrastructure lender.
eligibility: Recent graduates (bachelor's or master's) with strong quantitative skills and interest in infrastructure finance.
prepResources: AIIB's careers portal opens applications annually; case-interview practice for finance/consulting-style questions is useful prep.

title: OECD Internship Programme
org: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
description: A paid, often remote or hybrid internship contributing to the OECD Secretary-General's strategic policy projects across economics, education, and governance.
eligibility: Enrolled in or recently completed a relevant graduate program; rolling recruitment throughout the year.
prepResources: Roles are posted on the OECD careers site by directorate — following the specific directorate you're interested in helps with timing.

title: Google PhD Fellowship Program
description: A multi-year fellowship recognizing outstanding PhD students doing innovative research in computer science and related fields, paired with a Google Research mentor.
eligibility: Enrolled full-time in a PhD program at an eligible university, nominated through the university rather than applying directly.
prepResources: Check with your department whether your university participates, since nominations (not open applications) drive selection.

title: NVIDIA Graduate Fellowship Program
description: A research fellowship for PhD students in fields like AI, graphics, robotics, and systems, providing funding plus a paid internship at NVIDIA Research.
eligibility: PhD students who have completed at least one year of doctoral study in a relevant computing or engineering discipline.
prepResources: A strong publication record and a clear tie between your research and an NVIDIA Research lab's focus areas strengthens the application.

title: Humboldt Research Fellowship
description: A postdoctoral research fellowship enabling researchers of any nationality to conduct a self-chosen, long-term research project in Germany with a host of their choice.
eligibility: Completed a doctorate within the last four years (postdoctoral) or established academics; any discipline, any nationality.
prepResources: Securing a host researcher in Germany who agrees to sponsor the stay is the first and most important step before applying.

title: Einstein Fellowship (Einstein Forum)
description: A residential fellowship at Einstein's former summer house in Caputh, Germany, giving a scholar or professional time and space to pursue a project outside their usual field.
eligibility: Open to college graduates in any field, typically under roughly 35, proposing an interdisciplinary or unconventional project.
prepResources: The Forum values genuinely interdisciplinary proposals — a project that stretches beyond your usual training tends to stand out.

title: Breakthrough Junior Challenge
description: A global video competition inviting teens to explain a challenging concept in life sciences, physics, or mathematics in an engaging short film, with a major scholarship as the top prize.
eligibility: Ages 13-18, enrolled in school; entries submitted as a public video (no team size limit for production help, but one student presenter).
prepResources: Past winning videos are posted on the official site and are the best model for tone, length, and visual clarity.

title: The Earth Prize
description: A global environmental innovation competition for teenage teams to design solutions to real sustainability problems, with a large combined prize pool for students, mentors, and schools.
eligibility: Teams of 13-19 year olds working with a mentor/teacher; open to school teams worldwide.
prepResources: The Earth Prize publishes a shortlist of past winning projects that illustrate the level of feasibility and detail judges expect.

title: James Dyson Award
description: An international student design-engineering award recognizing inventions that solve real problems, judged first nationally and then by James Dyson himself at the international stage.
eligibility: Current or recent (within four years) students of product design, industrial design, or engineering.
prepResources: A working prototype and a clear explanation of the problem solved matter more than polish — review past national and international winners.

title: Conrad Challenge
description: A team-based STEM innovation challenge that takes student ventures from concept through a business pitch, culminating in an in-person Innovation Summit.
eligibility: Teams of 2-5 students aged roughly 13-18, working across categories like aerospace, cyber, energy, and health.
prepResources: The Challenge runs in stages (Innovation Summit stages), so pacing your team's proposal, prototype, and pitch materials to each deadline matters.

title: Solar Decathlon Design Challenge
description: A collegiate competition where student teams design high-performance, energy-efficient buildings, judged across categories from architecture to market potential.
eligibility: Teams of undergraduate or graduate students with a faculty advisor, typically an architecture or engineering program.
prepResources: The DOE publishes detailed rules and past team submissions each cycle — reviewing a previous division's winning entry helps calibrate scope.

title: Hello Tomorrow Global Challenge
description: A global deep-tech startup competition connecting founders working on hard-science solutions (energy, biotech, materials, etc.) with investors, corporates, and a large cash prize pool.
eligibility: Early-stage deep-tech startups, typically pre-Series B, applying with a working technology or strong prototype.
prepResources: Hello Tomorrow's challenge site lists sector tracks and past winners — tailoring the pitch to a specific track improves shortlisting odds.

title: MIT Solve Global Challenges
description: An open innovation platform that runs annual challenges (health, climate, economic prosperity, learning) and connects selected Solver teams with funding, mentorship, and MIT's network.
eligibility: Any individual or organization, anywhere in the world, with a tech-enabled solution addressing one of the year's challenge categories.
prepResources: MIT Solve publishes the specific challenge briefs each cycle; reading a prior year's Solver profiles clarifies the level of traction expected.

title: D-Prize
description: A competition funding entrepreneurs to distribute proven poverty interventions — like clean water, basic health products, or educational materials — into new communities.
eligibility: Applicants propose scaling an already-proven intervention (not a novel invention) via a for-profit or nonprofit venture.
prepResources: D-Prize publishes a list of the specific interventions it funds each cycle; matching a proposal tightly to one of these list items is key.

title: ClimateLaunchpad Green Business Idea Competition
description: The world's largest green business ideas competition, taking climate-tech founders from national heats through regional and global finals with pitch training along the way.
eligibility: Early-stage cleantech or climate-focused business ideas; entry typically happens through a national ClimateLaunchpad chapter.
prepResources: Find your country's local ClimateLaunchpad chapter first, since national heats determine who advances to the global final.
location: Global (national heats, global final in Europe)

title: Biomimicry Institute Ray of Hope Accelerator
description: A six-month accelerator for early-stage startups building nature-inspired solutions to environmental challenges, pairing founders with biomimicry experts and seed funding.
eligibility: Early-stage startups (pre-seed to seed) whose product or process is directly inspired by biological strategies.
prepResources: Review the Biomimicry Institute's design framework — being able to articulate the specific biological model behind your solution matters.

title: Google Summer of Code
description: A global program pairing students and new developers with open-source organizations for a paid, mentored coding project over the northern-hemisphere summer.
eligibility: Open to students and, in recent years, other newcomers to open source, aged 18 or older.
prepResources: Browse the participating organizations' project idea lists and make a small contribution before the application period to build rapport with mentors.

title: IBM Call for Code Global Challenge
description: A global hackathon-style challenge inviting developers to build open-source technology solutions to pressing humanitarian and climate problems, with a substantial prize pool.
eligibility: Open to individuals and teams of developers worldwide; solutions must be open source.
prepResources: IBM publishes starter kits and past winning solutions; using IBM Cloud/Watson tools referenced in the challenge brief is generally rewarded.

title: Google Code Jam
description: Google's long-running international algorithmic programming competition, run in timed online rounds that narrow down to an in-person world championship.
eligibility: Open to individuals aged 16 and older worldwide; no team requirement.
prepResources: Practice on Google's archive of past Code Jam problems, which mirror the style and difficulty of the qualification rounds.

title: Yale Young Global Scholars
description: A two-week, interdisciplinary summer academic program at Yale for high schoolers, combining seminar-style classes with leadership and college-readiness workshops.
eligibility: High schoolers roughly 16-18 years old (typically grades 10-11 equivalent); financial aid is available based on need.
prepResources: Choose a session track (there are several thematic options) that matches your genuine academic interest, since essays are track-specific.

title: PROMYS (Program in Mathematics for Young Scientists)
description: A six-week intensive summer mathematics program for motivated high schoolers, centered on discovery-based problem solving rather than lectures, fully funded for admitted students.
eligibility: High school students with strong problem-solving ability; the application itself is a substantial problem set.
prepResources: Practice with past PROMYS application problem sets, which are published on the program site and reward persistence over prior formal training.

title: Stanford Biodesign Innovation Fellowship
description: A ten-month, full-time fellowship training clinicians, engineers, and business professionals to identify unmet health needs and invent new medical technologies from scratch.
eligibility: Graduate degree in medicine, engineering, or business; teams are deliberately built to mix these backgrounds.
prepResources: Review the Biodesign process (need-finding, concept generation, prototyping) described on the program site before the interview stage.

title: Acumen Fellows Program
description: A year-long fellowship equipping emerging leaders to tackle poverty through moral leadership training, peer cohorts, and applied work on social enterprises, run in several regional tracks including India.
eligibility: Working professionals (often mid-career) already engaged in social-impact work; regional cohorts have their own specific criteria.
prepResources: Review which regional fellowship track (e.g., India, East Africa, Global) fits your location, since applications and timelines differ by region.

title: SBI Youth for India Fellowship
description: A 13-month fellowship placing young professionals with grassroots NGOs across rural India to work directly on development projects in health, education, livelihoods and the environment.
eligibility: Indian citizens aged 21-32 with a bachelor's degree, willing to relocate to a rural placement for the fellowship duration.
prepResources: The program pairs fellows with a specific NGO partner — reviewing the current year's list of partner NGOs helps target your application.

title: Goldman Sachs 10,000 Women
description: A global initiative providing business and management education, mentoring, and networking support to women entrepreneurs to help them scale their ventures.
eligibility: Women who own or co-own an existing small or growing business; programs are delivered through regional academic partners.
prepResources: Programs are often delivered locally through a university partner in your region — check Goldman Sachs' partner list for the current cycle.

title: Thomson Reuters Foundation Journalist Fellowship Programme
org: Thomson Reuters Foundation / University of Oxford
description: A fully funded fellowship at Oxford's Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism, giving working journalists three to six months to research and reflect on a topic in their field.
eligibility: Practicing journalists, typically mid-career, from any country, with English proficiency sufficient for academic study.
prepResources: A clear, tightly scoped research question about journalism practice (not just a general interest) is central to a strong proposal.

title: FASPE Fellowships
org: Fellowships at Auschwitz for the Study of Professional Ethics
description: A two-week program in Germany and Poland that takes early-career professionals from fields like law, medicine, journalism, and business through the history of the Holocaust to examine present-day professional ethics.
eligibility: Graduate students or early-career professionals in one of FASPE's specific professional tracks (law, medicine, journalism, business, clergy, design/technology).
prepResources: Choose the professional track that matches your actual field, since each track has a distinct itinerary and application essay prompts.

title: Atlantic Fellows for Social and Economic Equity
org: London School of Economics / Atlantic Institute
description: A fully funded year at LSE for professionals working to reduce social and economic inequality, combining an academic program with a global fellows network.
eligibility: Mid-career professionals with a demonstrated track record addressing inequality, from any country or sector.
prepResources: The program looks for practitioners already working on inequality issues, so grounding the application in real project experience matters most.

title: BeVisioneers: The Mercedes-Benz Fellowship
description: A twelve-month program training young people to design and pursue sustainability-focused projects, combining mentorship from industry experts with a peer community of fellows worldwide.
eligibility: Ages roughly 16-28, from any country, with an early-stage idea or interest in environmental sustainability.
prepResources: The application typically asks for a specific sustainability project idea — a concrete, locally grounded idea outperforms a broad concept.

title: UNEP Young Champions of the Earth
description: UNEP's flagship award for environmental entrepreneurship, recognizing young innovators whose ventures address pressing ecological challenges with seed funding and mentorship.
eligibility: Ages 18-30 with an existing environmental project or business, applying by region.
prepResources: UNEP evaluates on impact and scalability of an existing initiative — having measurable early results strengthens an application significantly.

title: Future for Nature Award
description: An annual award honoring young conservationists worldwide for concrete, measurable contributions to protecting endangered species and ecosystems, with substantial follow-on funding.
eligibility: Ages roughly 18-35 with a demonstrated track record of species or habitat conservation work.
prepResources: Nominations and self-applications both require concrete evidence of conservation outcomes, not just intent or plans.

title: Vital Voices Global Fellowship
description: A ten-month fellowship for established women leaders across sectors, providing skills training, mentorship, and access to a global network built to expand their leadership impact.
eligibility: Women leaders with an established track record in their field; specific tracks vary by cohort (e.g., economic empowerment, human rights).
prepResources: Vital Voices runs several distinct fellowship tracks — identify the one matching your existing work before applying.

title: Inlaks Shivdasani Foundation Scholarships
description: Loan-based scholarships supporting Indian students pursuing full-time postgraduate study, research, or professional training abroad in almost any field.
eligibility: Indian citizens under 30 admitted to a full-time postgraduate program at a recognized foreign institution.
prepResources: Secure your international university admission before applying — the Foundation requires proof of acceptance as part of the process.

title: J.N. Tata Endowment Scholarship
description: A century-old, interest-free loan scholarship supporting Indian students pursuing postgraduate, doctoral, or research study abroad across virtually any discipline.
eligibility: Indian citizens who have secured admission to a recognized postgraduate program outside India.
prepResources: Applications require a confirmed foreign university admission letter, so apply to universities first and to the Endowment once accepted.

title: Narotam Sekhsaria Scholarship Programme
description: An interest-free loan scholarship with mentoring support for Indian students pursuing postgraduate study either in India or abroad.
eligibility: Indian citizens with an admission offer to a recognized postgraduate program; strong academic record required.
prepResources: The Foundation pairs recipients with mentors, so being ready to articulate long-term career goals in the interview stage helps.

title: UNESCO World Heritage Young Professionals Forum
description: A fully funded forum bringing young professionals together in Paris to discuss the intersection of world heritage conservation and emerging digital technologies.
eligibility: Ages roughly 23-32 with a background or strong interest in heritage conservation, architecture, or related fields.
prepResources: A concrete idea for applying digital tools to a specific heritage site or challenge strengthens the required proposal.

title: Non-Trivial Fellowship
description: An online fellowship supporting ambitious teenagers and young adults to pursue independent research, policy, or entrepreneurial projects with direct funding and mentorship.
eligibility: Ages roughly 14-20, from any country, with a genuine project idea rather than an existing organization.
prepResources: Applications favor a specific, well-reasoned project proposal over a general statement of interest in the fellowship's themes.

title: Global Undergraduate Exchange Program (Global UGRAD)
description: A fully funded, non-degree semester of study at a U.S. university and college for undergraduates, including community engagement and internship components.
eligibility: Current undergraduate students from eligible countries, nominated through the U.S. Embassy or a partner organization in their country.
prepResources: Applications typically route through your national U.S. Embassy's education office rather than directly to the program site.

title: CERN Beamline for Schools Competition
description: An international competition where teams of high schoolers design a real particle-physics experiment, with winning teams traveling to run their experiment on an actual CERN beamline.
eligibility: Teams of at least five high school students aged 16 and above with a teacher/coach; open worldwide.
prepResources: CERN provides a detailed technical proposal template and past winning proposals, which are the best guide to the expected level of rigor.

title: The Geneva Challenge
description: An annual team competition inviting graduate students worldwide to propose pragmatic, evidence-based solutions to a pressing global development problem, judged by an expert panel in Geneva.
eligibility: Teams of 3-5 current graduate students from any university worldwide.
prepResources: The Challenge sets a new annual theme with a detailed brief — closely following the brief's specific evaluation criteria matters more than originality alone.

title: World Food Forum Youth Policy and Deliberation Challenge
description: A policy-proposal challenge inviting young people to develop concrete recommendations on agrifood systems and climate resilience, feeding into the World Food Forum's youth-led policy work.
eligibility: Ages roughly 18-35, individually or in teams, from any country.
prepResources: Reviewing prior years' Youth Policy Board recommendations helps calibrate the level of specificity FAO expects from proposals.

title: RISE (Rhodes Trust / Schmidt Futures)
description: A global talent search identifying teenagers for a lifetime of support — scholarships, mentorship, and community — aimed at helping them pursue work that benefits others.
eligibility: Ages 15-17 from any country, regardless of academic record or financial background.
prepResources: RISE explicitly looks beyond grades — a genuine project or initiative you've already started, however small, is a strong signal.

title: IIASA Young Scientists Summer Program
org: International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis
description: A three-month residency at IIASA's Austria headquarters where PhD students apply systems-analysis methods to global sustainability challenges alongside senior researchers.
eligibility: PhD students (typically within their first few years) in a field relevant to systems analysis, environment, or policy.
prepResources: Applicants propose a research topic aligned with an IIASA program area, so reading current program research agendas beforehand helps.

title: Mitacs Globalink Research Award
description: A 12-week funded research internship for undergraduates at Canadian universities, working directly with a Canadian faculty supervisor on an original research project.
eligibility: Undergraduate students at partner institutions in eligible countries with strong academic standing.
prepResources: Match your research interests to a specific Canadian faculty supervisor's published work before applying, since projects are supervisor-led.

title: The Asia Foundation Development Fellows Program
description: A leadership development fellowship for professionals under 40 working across Asia on governance, development, or social-impact challenges, combining regional and U.S.-based components.
eligibility: Professionals under 40 based in Asia with a track record of leadership in their organization or community.
prepResources: The Foundation looks for evidence of leadership already exercised in your current role, not just potential.
location: Multiple countries in Asia and the US

title: International Women's Media Foundation Fund for Women Journalists
description: A grant supporting women journalists' reporting projects or professional development, particularly those covering underreported stories of global importance.
eligibility: Women-identifying journalists of any nationality with a specific reporting project or professional development need.
prepResources: IWMF publishes grant categories and past supported projects; matching your pitch closely to a currently open grant category is important.

title: Harry Frank Guggenheim Foundation Distinguished Scholars Award
description: A substantial multi-year research award funding scholars and institutions studying the causes, manifestations, and control of violence.
eligibility: Established researchers or research institutions with a proposed program of study on violence and its social dimensions.
prepResources: The Foundation funds programmatic, multi-year research agendas rather than single studies — proposals should reflect that scope.

title: TED Fellows Program
description: A program that identifies innovators doing exceptional work across science, technology, art, and social impact, offering them a TED stage, public-speaking coaching, and a lasting peer community.
eligibility: Individuals of any age or nationality already doing distinctive, impactful work in their field, nominated or self-applying.
prepResources: The application asks you to describe your work's originality and impact concisely — TED's own Fellows profiles are a useful benchmark.

title: Global Solutions Initiative Young Global Changers Award
description: An award recognizing young people whose projects align economic growth with environmental and social sustainability, with winners invited to the Global Solutions Summit in Berlin.
eligibility: Ages roughly 18-35 with an existing project addressing sustainable economic transformation.
prepResources: The award favors projects with measurable early traction over concept-stage ideas; concrete metrics strengthen the entry.

title: Amazon Future Engineer Scholarship (India)
description: A scholarship supporting academically strong, financially underserved students — with a particular focus on girls — through undergraduate engineering or computer science study in India.
eligibility: Admitted to or enrolled in an engineering/CS undergraduate program in India, with demonstrated financial need.
prepResources: Have income proof and admission documents ready, since the scholarship verifies financial need each renewal year.

title: L'Oréal India For Young Women in Science Scholarship
description: A scholarship for female undergraduate and postgraduate students pursuing scientific fields, intended to counter the gender gap in STEM higher education.
eligibility: Female Indian students admitted to a science degree program, generally with defined household-income limits.
prepResources: Recommendation letters emphasizing research aptitude or lab experience strengthen the application.

title: Bharti Airtel Scholarship
description: A need-based scholarship covering tuition and living costs for first-year undergraduates from low-income households entering engineering, science, or technology programs.
eligibility: First-year undergraduates in India from families below a defined income threshold, admitted to a recognized institution.
prepResources: Applications typically open right after board results — have admission proof and income certificates ready in advance.

title: Kotak Kanya Scholarship
description: A scholarship for meritorious female undergraduates from economically disadvantaged families across India, covering a portion of annual tuition.
eligibility: Female undergraduates in India with strong prior academic marks and family income below a set threshold.
prepResources: Prior-year mark sheets and income certificates are usually required upfront in the application form.

title: Legrand Empowering Scholarship Program
description: A scholarship for academically strong female high school graduates in India pursuing commerce or science-stream undergraduate degrees.
eligibility: Female students in India who have completed class 12 in a commerce or science stream with strong academic results.
prepResources: Have your class 12 mark sheet and a clear course-admission plan ready before the application window opens.

title: Raman Kant Munjal Scholarship
description: A need-based scholarship supporting students from lower-income families in India who are pursuing finance-related undergraduate degrees.
eligibility: Class 12 graduates in India with family income below a defined threshold, admitted to a finance-related course.
prepResources: Income and admission proof are the two documents most commonly requested — gather these ahead of time.

title: Reliance Foundation Scholarships
description: Merit- and means-based scholarships for first-year Indian undergraduate and postgraduate students, awarded through an aptitude test and academic review.
eligibility: First-year undergraduate or postgraduate students in India with strong class 12/degree results.
prepResources: The Foundation runs its own aptitude test as part of screening, so practicing general reasoning/aptitude questions helps.

title: HDFC Parivartan ECSS Scholarship
description: A need-based scholarship for meritorious school and college students from low-income households across India, part of HDFC's broader education-access initiative.
eligibility: School or college students in India with strong academics and demonstrated financial need.
prepResources: Applications are usually processed through the bank's CSR portal; keep income and school-enrollment documents ready.

title: College Board India Scholars Program
description: A program waiving SAT fees and, for top scorers from lower-income families, providing full-tuition scholarships at partner universities for Indian students.
eligibility: Indian students in grades 11-12 from families below a set income threshold who achieve a strong SAT score (historically 1300+).
prepResources: Register early for the fee waiver before taking the SAT, since the scholarship track depends on your official score.

title: Meta PhD Fellowship
description: A fellowship recognizing PhD students doing innovative research in computer science and engineering, including a funded visit to a Meta research lab and mentorship from Meta researchers.
eligibility: Enrolled full-time in a PhD program at an accredited university, nominated by their department.
prepResources: Check with your department whether it participates in Meta's fellowship nomination process, since this isn't an open individual application.

title: Google Kick Start Coding Competition
description: A global online algorithmic programming competition run in timed rounds, open to coders of all levels and used by Google as a talent pipeline for interviews.
eligibility: Open to anyone aged 16 or older worldwide; no team or academic prerequisites.
prepResources: Google's archive of past Kick Start problems by round is the best practice material, alongside general competitive-programming platforms.

title: NASA Deep Space Food Challenge
description: A challenge inviting teams to design novel food-production technologies capable of feeding astronauts on years-long deep space missions with minimal resource input.
eligibility: Open to individuals, teams, and organizations worldwide; no formal food-science credential required.
prepResources: Review NASA's published technical requirements around mass, volume, and nutrition per crew member before designing a system.

title: NASA CineSpace Short Film Competition
description: A short film competition requiring entrants to incorporate real NASA footage or imagery into an original narrative, screened at a Houston film festival.
eligibility: Open to filmmakers of any experience level worldwide; films must creatively incorporate NASA's public media archive.
prepResources: Browse NASA's public image and video libraries first — footage selection often shapes the story more than the reverse.

title: Wharton Global Youth Program
description: An online business-education program for high schoolers covering entrepreneurship, finance, and management fundamentals taught by Wharton faculty.
eligibility: High school students, typically grades 9-12, from any country.
prepResources: Need-based financial aid is available — apply for it in the same window as the program itself if cost is a barrier.

title: Vienna BioCenter Summer School
description: A fully funded nine-week hands-on life-sciences research program in Vienna, pairing undergraduates with active research groups for a real lab project.
eligibility: Undergraduate students in biology, biochemistry, or a related life-science field with some lab experience.
prepResources: A short research-interest statement matching your background to a specific VBC lab's focus strengthens the application.

title: ETH Zurich Student Summer Research Fellowship
description: A two-month fellowship giving undergraduates their first hands-on research experience in computer science, working directly with an ETH Zurich research group.
eligibility: Undergraduate students in computer science or a closely related field, generally in their second year or later.
prepResources: Identify a specific ETH CS research group whose published work matches your interests before writing the motivation statement.

title: HZDR Summer Student Program
description: A six- to nine-week summer research placement in Germany for science and engineering students, working on live projects in physics, materials, or nuclear science.
eligibility: Undergraduate or early graduate students in physics, engineering, or a related natural-science field.
prepResources: HZDR lists current project topics by research division — pick one that matches your coursework before applying.

title: International Youth Math Challenge (IYMC)
description: A multi-round online mathematics competition open to school and college students worldwide, progressing from qualifiers through pre-finals to a final round.
eligibility: School or college students of any nationality; no prior competition experience required to enter qualifiers.
prepResources: Past qualifier and final-round problem sets are published on the IYMC site and are the best practice material.

title: International Online Physics Competition (Physics Brawl)
org: Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports, Czech Republic
description: A team-based online physics problem-solving competition run over several hours, testing both depth of physics knowledge and teamwork under time pressure.
eligibility: High school students worldwide, competing in teams of up to five.
prepResources: The organizers publish archives of past competitions' problems and solutions, which are the standard prep material.

title: UN Millennium Fellowship
org: United Nations Academic Impact / Millennium Campus Network
description: A semester-long leadership program where cohorts of student fellows on a single campus design and run a project tackling one of the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
eligibility: Current undergraduate students at a participating campus; cohorts are formed by campus, not individually.
prepResources: Check whether your university already has an active Millennium Fellowship cohort, since applications are managed at the campus level.

title: IBM Master the Mainframe Challenge
description: A three-level self-paced learning competition teaching mainframe computing fundamentals, with travel stipends and scholarships awarded to top performers.
eligibility: Students aged 13 and older worldwide; no prior mainframe experience needed to start at Level 1.
prepResources: IBM provides free self-paced tutorials for each level — working through Level 1 fully before attempting later levels is the standard approach.

title: Samsung Solve for Tomorrow
description: A competition challenging students to design a product, app, or service idea that solves a real-world community problem, with incubation support for finalist teams.
eligibility: School students within a defined age range (varies by country edition), individually or in small teams.
prepResources: Samsung publishes past winning ideas by country — a solution grounded in a specific, local, verifiable problem tends to score better than a generic concept.

title: Microsoft Imagine Cup
description: A global student technology competition where teams build a working product using Microsoft's cloud and AI tools, with mentorship and a path to Microsoft's startup programs.
eligibility: University students aged 16 and older, working individually or in teams of up to four.
prepResources: Microsoft provides free Azure credits to participants — building and testing an early prototype before the submission deadline is expected.

title: The New York Times Learning Network Student Contests
description: An ongoing series of free writing, video, and multimedia contests for teenagers — including personal narrative, editorial, review, podcast, and STEM-explainer formats — with winning entries published by the Times.
eligibility: Students roughly aged 11-19 worldwide; each contest format has its own specific age range and word/time limit.
prepResources: The Learning Network publishes past winning entries for every contest format, which is the clearest guide to tone and structure expected.

title: J-PAL Research for Impact Fellowship
description: A fellowship training aspiring development economists in rigorous impact-evaluation methods through an MITx MicroMasters credential followed by field-based research work in India.
eligibility: Recent graduates or graduate students with strong quantitative training, interested in development-economics research.
prepResources: Completing (or starting) the MITx Data, Economics, and Development Policy MicroMasters ahead of time strengthens an application significantly.

title: Harvard International Review Writing Contest
description: A short-form (roughly 800-1200 word) writing contest for high schoolers on international-affairs topics, with finalists invited to a virtual defense of their argument.
eligibility: High school students (roughly grades 9-12) worldwide.
prepResources: HIR's own published articles are the best model for the analytical, evidence-driven tone the judges expect.

title: Stanford Pre-Collegiate Summer Institutes
description: An online summer learning program for high schoolers built around critical thinking and interdisciplinary problem solving rather than a single subject track.
eligibility: High schoolers roughly in grades 8-11; need-based financial aid is available.
prepResources: Course descriptions vary by summer — choosing based on genuine intellectual interest rather than perceived prestige tends to produce a stronger application essay.

title: Watson Semester Accelerator Program
description: A four-month, in-person entrepreneurship accelerator in Boulder, Colorado, giving young founders hands-on mentorship and a small peer cohort to launch a real venture.
eligibility: College-age students or recent graduates with an early-stage venture idea; cohort sizes are intentionally small.
prepResources: Applicants should come with more than an idea — some early validation (a prototype, waitlist, or pilot customer) strengthens the case.

title: We Empower UN SDG Challenge
description: A challenge recognizing women-led businesses that advance the UN Sustainable Development Goals through their core commercial operations, with a cash grant opportunity for finalists.
eligibility: Women entrepreneurs or women-led businesses with an operating model that advances one or more SDGs.
prepResources: Applications ask for concrete business metrics tied to SDG impact, not just mission statements — have revenue/impact data ready.

title: World Economic Forum Early Careers Programme
description: A six-month, paid early-career program placing recent graduates in WEF offices worldwide across research, partnerships, and communications functions.
eligibility: Recent university graduates with strong academic records and an interest in global governance or policy work.
prepResources: WEF's careers page lists specific open roles by office and function — tailoring your application to a named role improves your odds.

title: Sir Harry Evans Global Fellowship in Investigative Journalism
description: A nine-month fellowship pairing early-career journalists with Reuters mentors to build investigative-reporting skills, combining newsroom placement with academic study at Durham.
eligibility: Working journalists with roughly two to five years of professional experience.
prepResources: A strong portfolio of published investigative or accountability journalism is the single most important application component.

title: CNN Academy Climate Storytelling Program
description: A media training program for young professionals culminating in the production of an original climate-crisis documentary under mentorship from CNN journalists.
eligibility: Early-career journalists or storytellers with an interest in climate and environmental reporting.
prepResources: A concrete story idea grounded in a specific community or region tends to stand out more than a broad climate-topic pitch.

title: Smart India Hackathon
description: India's largest national hackathon, where student teams build working solutions to real problem statements submitted by government ministries and industry partners over a 36-hour build.
eligibility: Teams of Indian college students, typically six per team with two mentors, registered through their institution.
prepResources: Problem statements are published weeks in advance on the SIH portal — picking one that matches your team's existing skills is critical given the short build window.

title: PROMYS India
description: A fully funded five-week intensive mathematics research program at IISc Bangalore, modeled on the original PROMYS program at Boston University, built around discovery-based problem sets.
eligibility: Indian students roughly in grades 9-12 with strong problem-solving ability; the application itself is a substantial math problem set.
prepResources: Work through PROMYS India's published application problem sets — persistence on hard problems matters more than prior formal training.

title: Mongabay Y. Eva Tan Conservation Reporting Fellowship
description: A six-month fellowship building environmental-journalism capacity in biodiversity-rich regions, pairing fellows with editorial mentorship at a leading conservation news outlet.
eligibility: Early-career journalists based in or reporting on biodiversity hotspot regions.
prepResources: A specific, locally reported story pitch (not just a general interest in conservation) is central to a competitive application.

title: Obama Foundation Voyager Scholarship
description: A scholarship and summer travel-fellowship for undergraduates committed to public service, combining tuition support with a funded storytelling trip abroad.
eligibility: Undergraduate students in the U.S. with demonstrated financial need and a public-service project or goal.
prepResources: A concrete public-service project already underway (not just an interest area) is central to the required application essay.

title: Rise Global Winners Program (Rhodes Trust / Schmidt Futures)
description: A continuation track for RISE-recognized teenagers, offering ongoing mentorship, funding for independent projects, and access to a lifelong support network.
eligibility: Teenagers who have been recognized through the RISE talent search process.
prepResources: This track builds on the initial RISE application — a specific, already-scoped independent project proposal is what advances candidates.

title: Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) Youth Business Challenge
description: A regional pitch competition for young entrepreneurs from APEC member economies, connecting early-stage founders with cross-border mentorship and investor exposure.
eligibility: Young entrepreneurs (typically under 35) based in an APEC member economy with an operating early-stage venture.
prepResources: APEC runs this challenge through member-economy business associations, so check your country's local APEC business chapter for the current cycle.

title: UNESCO-UNEVOC Skills in Action Photo Competition
description: A photography competition documenting technical and vocational skills development around the world, intended to highlight the human side of workforce training.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any age or nationality worldwide.
prepResources: Photos that show a specific person practicing a specific skill (rather than abstract classroom shots) tend to score better with judges.

title: Breakthrough Generation Fellowship
description: A ten-week paid fellowship in Berkeley, California, training college students in environmental and energy policy through an ecomodernist lens, including original research and writing.
eligibility: Current college students or recent graduates with strong interest in energy, climate, or environmental policy.
prepResources: Familiarity with the Breakthrough Institute's published research and general policy stance is genuinely useful for the interview stage.

title: Vivekananda Fellowship
description: A two-year residential leadership fellowship in India training recent graduates in values-based leadership through structured coursework, service work, and mentorship.
eligibility: Indian college graduates willing to commit to a two-year residential program.
prepResources: This is a values- and service-oriented program — prior volunteering or community work is more relevant than academic credentials alone.

title: Dalai Lama Fellows Program
description: A yearlong fellowship combining contemplative leadership training with remote project work, helping young leaders build socially engaged initiatives grounded in compassion-based practice.
eligibility: Young people roughly aged 20-36 with an existing or emerging community project.
prepResources: The fellowship blends contemplative practice with project work — being able to describe your project's community impact concretely matters most.

title: Goonj Grassroots Fellowship
description: A yearlong fellowship immersing young people directly in grassroots community work across urban and rural India, focused on dignity-based development approaches.
eligibility: Indian citizens aged 21-35 willing to work on-ground in community settings for the full fellowship term.
prepResources: Goonj's own published case studies on dignity-based development give a strong sense of the organization's philosophy before applying.

title: Bhumi Fellowship
description: A two-year paid fellowship placing graduates on school-transformation projects in Chennai, working directly with under-resourced government schools.
eligibility: College graduates, generally with Tamil language fluency given the on-ground community work involved.
prepResources: Prior volunteering experience with Bhumi or a similar education-access nonprofit strengthens an application significantly.

title: Davis Fellows for Peace (Middlebury Language Schools)
description: A fellowship fully covering the cost of an intensive summer language-immersion program at Middlebury's Language Schools for students committed to peacebuilding work.
eligibility: Undergraduate students with a demonstrated interest or project in peace and conflict-resolution work.
prepResources: Middlebury looks for a genuine tie between the language you'd study and a specific peace-related goal, not language study for its own sake.

title: Girl Up Teen Advisor Program
description: A yearlong leadership program training teenage girls in gender-equality advocacy, public speaking, and campaign organizing alongside a global peer cohort.
eligibility: Teenage girls roughly aged 14-18 from any country with an interest in gender-equality advocacy.
prepResources: Prior involvement in a school or community Girl Up club, or any advocacy project, strengthens the application meaningfully.

title: 1000 Girls, 1000 Futures Program
description: A yearlong STEM mentorship program pairing teenage girls with women professionals in science and technology fields for structured, virtual mentorship.
eligibility: Girls roughly aged 13-17 worldwide with an interest in STEM fields.
prepResources: The application usually asks about a specific STEM interest area, which helps match mentees with a relevant mentor.

title: UNESCO Clubs Worldwide Multimedia Contest
description: A multimedia (video, essay, or art) competition inviting youth to propose ways their community can advance a specific UN Sustainable Development Goal, with a US-based youth camp invitation for top entries.
eligibility: Ages roughly 10-24 worldwide, submitting individually or as a group.
prepResources: Grounding the entry in a specific, local SDG-related action (not an abstract statement) is what distinguishes winning entries.

title: Ayn Rand Institute Essay Contests (Anthem, Fountainhead, Atlas Shrugged)
description: A set of annual essay contests open to students on Ayn Rand's novels Anthem, The Fountainhead, and Atlas Shrugged, each with a tiered cash prize pool and separate age brackets.
eligibility: Grade brackets vary by novel (roughly grades 8-12 for Anthem/Fountainhead, high school and college for Atlas Shrugged).
prepResources: The Institute publishes the specific prompt and word limit for each novel's contest separately every year — read the current prompt closely before drafting.

title: The Queen's Commonwealth Essay Competition
description: The world's oldest international schools' writing competition, inviting essays on Commonwealth-related themes across junior and senior age categories, with winners honored at a London ceremony.
eligibility: School students under 18, from any Commonwealth or non-Commonwealth country, in junior and senior brackets.
prepResources: The RCS publishes past winning essays, which are a strong model for the historical/cultural depth expected.

title: Foyle Young Poets of the Year Award
description: One of the world's largest poetry competitions for young writers, open on any theme, with roughly 100 winners receiving prizes, mentoring, and publication in an anthology.
eligibility: Ages 11-17, from any country, submitting up to two original poems.
prepResources: The Poetry Society publishes past winning anthologies free online — reading a few is the best way to gauge the judges' taste.

title: Fitzwilliam College Essay Competitions
description: A set of subject-specific essay competitions (across humanities and social sciences) for pre-university students, with cash prizes and feedback from Cambridge academics.
eligibility: Students in their final two years of secondary school (roughly ages 16-18), from any country.
prepResources: Each subject essay competition sets its own specific question — closely answering the set question matters more than general subject knowledge.

title: Trinity College Cambridge Essay Prizes
description: A series of subject essay prizes (law, history, literature, linguistics) for secondary school students, judged by Trinity College fellows, with the prize split between student and school.
eligibility: Students in the final two years of secondary school (roughly grades 11-12), from any country.
prepResources: Trinity publishes each year's specific set question by subject — engaging directly and critically with that question is essential.

title: St Hugh's College Sixth Form Essay Competitions
description: A set of multi-subject essay competitions for secondary school students on Oxford-style set questions, offering cash prizes and academic feedback.
eligibility: Students in their final two years of secondary school (roughly grades 11-12), any country.
prepResources: Each subject has its own prompt published on the college's outreach page — treat it like an Oxford-style tutorial question, not a generic essay.

title: Oxford Practical Ethics Competition
org: University of Oxford (Uehiro Centre for Practical Ethics)
description: A team video-submission competition for secondary students on a contemporary ethics question, culminating in a live debate day hosted by Oxford's Practical Ethics team.
eligibility: Secondary school students, roughly ages 15-18, working in small teams.
prepResources: Past years' set questions and winning video submissions are published on the Centre's site as a guide to expected depth and format.

title: Woolf Essay Prize
description: An essay prize for female secondary school students responding to an annually set prompt, named for Virginia Woolf, with book-token prizes for top entries.
eligibility: Female students in their final two years of secondary school (roughly grade 11-12).
prepResources: The set prompt changes each year and is published on Newnham's site — read it carefully rather than adapting a stock essay.

title: Cambridge Re:think Essay Competition
description: An essay competition spanning moral, political, scientific, and philosophical prompts for secondary school students, with a substantial combined cash prize pool.
eligibility: Secondary school students roughly aged 11-18, in junior and senior categories.
prepResources: Prompts span multiple disciplines each year — pick the specific question that best matches genuine subject interest rather than perceived ease.

title: Jane Austen Society of North America Essay Contest
org: Jane Austen Society of North America (JASNA)
description: A literary-analysis essay contest on Jane Austen's novels across three academic levels (high school, college, graduate), with cash prizes for each category.
eligibility: Currently enrolled high school, undergraduate, or graduate students, from any country.
prepResources: JASNA sets a specific annual prompt tied to one Austen novel — close textual analysis matters more than broad biographical context.

title: American Society of Human Genetics DNA Day Essay Contest
description: An essay contest for high schoolers on a genetics-related prompt tied to National DNA Day, judged by genetics professionals with cash prizes for top entries.
eligibility: Students in grades 9-12, from any country, writing individually.
prepResources: ASHG publishes the annual prompt and past winning essays — accuracy on the underlying genetics matters as much as writing quality.

title: World Trade Organization Essay Award for Young Economists
description: An essay award encouraging original economic research on international trade policy, open to PhD students and young researchers, with a cash prize and possible publication.
eligibility: PhD students or researchers within a few years of completing a doctorate, writing on a trade-policy topic.
prepResources: The WTO's own working paper series is useful background reading to pitch a genuinely novel angle rather than a well-trodden topic.

title: World History Association Student Essay Contest
description: An essay contest inviting secondary students to reflect on the value of studying world history, with a cash prize for the top entry.
eligibility: Secondary school students of any nationality.
prepResources: Judges favor essays grounded in a specific historical example over broad, abstract claims about history's importance.

title: Global Peter Drucker Forum Essay Contest
description: An essay contest on management, leadership, and economic themes tied to the annual Peter Drucker Forum in Vienna, with cash prizes and a funded trip to the conference for winners.
eligibility: Ages roughly 18-35, from any country, submitting an original essay on the year's forum theme.
prepResources: The Forum publishes its annual theme well ahead of the deadline — tying the essay concretely to that theme is essential.

title: Nikon Small World Photomicrography Competition
description: The world's leading photomicrography competition, recognizing the best images and videos captured through a light microscope, with a substantial grand prize.
eligibility: Open to anyone using microscopy, from hobbyists to professional researchers, any country.
prepResources: Past winners are archived on the Small World site, illustrating the technical quality and originality judges look for.

title: Sony World Photography Awards
description: One of the largest global photography competitions, spanning professional, open, student, and youth categories, with winners announced at a London ceremony and gear prizes.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any level worldwide; separate tracks exist for students and under-19 entrants.
prepResources: Choose the specific category (Open, Professional, Student, Youth) that matches your body of work, since judging criteria differ by track.

title: Smithsonian Magazine Photo Contest
description: An annual photography contest across categories like natural world, travel, and people, with a notable grand prize and category winners featured in the magazine.
eligibility: Photographers aged 18 and older, from any country.
prepResources: Smithsonian publishes past winning galleries by category — reviewing them clarifies the level of technical polish expected.

title: Banff Mountain Photo Essay Contest
org: Banff Centre Mountain Film and Book Festival
description: A photo-essay competition (3-5 images with narrative) capturing mountain culture, adventure sport, or wildlife, judged as part of Canada's renowned mountain festival.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any level worldwide submitting a themed set of mountain-related images.
prepResources: A strong photo essay tells a coherent narrative across the set, not just five strong standalone images — plan the sequence deliberately.

title: Royal Society of Biology Photography Competition
description: An annual biology-themed photography competition with junior and open categories, celebrating images that capture the diversity of life sciences.
eligibility: Open to all ages, with a dedicated under-18 category.
prepResources: A clear biological subject and strong technical composition both matter — the RSB publishes judging criteria on its site each cycle.

title: Environmental Photographer of the Year
org: Chartered Institution of Water and Environmental Management (CIWEM)
description: A photography award recognizing images that capture the human relationship with the environment, spanning categories from climate change to conservation.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any level worldwide.
prepResources: CIWEM's category descriptions each year favor a clear environmental narrative over purely aesthetic nature photography.

title: Current World Archaeology (CWA) Photo Competition
description: A photography competition celebrating archaeological sites and heritage worldwide, with winners receiving conference passes and a magazine feature.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any level aged 18 and older.
prepResources: Photos of well-known sites need a distinctive angle or moment to stand out — the magazine's past winners gallery shows what works.

title: Andrei Stenin International Press Photo Contest
description: A photojournalism competition for young photographers documenting current events, spanning news, sports, and portrait categories, with a substantial cash grand prize.
eligibility: Photographers aged 18-33, from any country, submitting original photojournalism work.
prepResources: Strong entries typically come from photographers with an existing portfolio of published photojournalism, not staged photography.

title: 500px Global Photography Awards
description: An annual photography competition across six genre categories, judged by industry photographers, with cash prizes for category winners.
eligibility: Open to 500px members of any skill level worldwide.
prepResources: Submissions must come from an existing 500px account — building a portfolio on the platform ahead of the deadline is worthwhile.

title: Standard Chartered Weather Photographer of the Year
description: A weather and climate photography competition across multiple categories including a youth award, celebrating images of meteorological phenomena worldwide.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any age worldwide; a specific youth category exists for under-18 entrants.
prepResources: A clear meteorological subject (storms, clouds, extreme weather) with a strong caption explaining the phenomenon strengthens entries.

title: American Meteorological Society Photo Contest
description: A photography competition capturing weather, water, and climate phenomena, judged by atmospheric scientists, with modest cash prizes for winners.
eligibility: Open to photographers aged 13 and older worldwide.
prepResources: A short, scientifically accurate caption describing the weather phenomenon shown is part of what judges evaluate.

title: Astronomy Photographer of the Year
description: One of the world's leading astrophotography competitions, spanning categories from deep sky to people-and-space, with substantial cash prizes and a London exhibition.
eligibility: Open to photographers of any level worldwide; separate junior category exists for under-16 entrants.
prepResources: Technical EXIF data (exposure, equipment) is often required at submission — keep detailed shooting notes for your entry.

title: Sony Future Filmmaker Awards
description: A short-film competition across genre categories including a dedicated student track, with a funded workshop trip to Sony Pictures Studios for finalists.
eligibility: Open to filmmakers of any experience level worldwide; a specific student category exists for enrolled students.
prepResources: Sony's category-specific rules (runtime, genre, content restrictions) are strict — read the current year's rulebook fully before submitting.
location: Global (online); workshop in Los Angeles, USA

title: Yale Drama Series Playwriting Competition
description: A playwriting competition for early-career writers judged by a working playwright, with the winning full-length play published by Yale University Press and given a professional staged reading.
eligibility: Playwrights who have not had a professionally produced play, from any country, submitting an unpublished full-length script.
prepResources: Read the judging playwright's own published work each cycle, since their sensibility strongly shapes what advances.

title: Depict! Short Film Competition
description: A short film competition capping entries at 90 seconds across any genre, screened at UK festivals, with a modest cash prize pool.
eligibility: Open to filmmakers of any level worldwide; entries must be 90 seconds or shorter.
prepResources: The tight runtime rewards a single strong idea executed cleanly over an ambitious plot that gets rushed.

title: HG Wells Short Story Competition
description: An annual themed short-story competition for young writers under 21, with a notable cash grand prize and publication opportunity.
eligibility: Writers under 21 years old, from any country, submitting an original short story (typically 1,500-5,000 words) on the year's theme.
prepResources: The organizers set a new theme each year and publish past winning stories, which are useful models for tone and structure.

title: Toyota Logistic Design Competition
description: A design competition inviting student teams to propose 2D or 3D circular-economy object designs addressing transportation and material-use challenges.
eligibility: College students, individually or in teams, from any country.
prepResources: Toyota's brief emphasizes sustainability and circular-economy thinking — grounding a design in a specific material-reuse case strengthens the entry.

title: Global Canvas Children's Art Competition
description: A children's art competition spanning painting, sculpture, textile, and collage on an annual theme, with cash or supply-voucher prizes for winners.
eligibility: Children roughly aged 4-16, from any country, submitting original artwork on the current theme.
prepResources: Global Canvas publishes past winning submissions by age group, which help calibrate the expected level of originality and technique.

title: Stanford Center on Longevity Design Challenge
description: A design competition for student teams proposing products or services that improve healthy aging and human longevity, judged in a final pitch round.
eligibility: College students worldwide, individually or in teams.
prepResources: Stanford's challenge brief changes focus each cycle — reading the specific year's brief closely is essential before designing a solution.

title: Commonwealth Professional Fellowships
description: A short-term professional development placement (six weeks to three months) in the UK for mid-career professionals from Commonwealth developing countries.
eligibility: Mid-career professionals from eligible Commonwealth countries with several years of relevant work experience.
prepResources: A UK host institution must be identified as part of the application, so researching potential hosts early is important.

title: Commonwealth Master's Scholarships
description: Full scholarships for master's study at UK universities for students from developing Commonwealth countries, covering tuition, travel, and living costs.
eligibility: Citizens of eligible developing Commonwealth countries with an undergraduate degree and a UK university offer.
prepResources: Secure a UK university offer first — the scholarship application usually happens after or alongside that offer.

title: German Chancellor Fellowship
description: A twelve-month fellowship funding a self-designed project year in Germany for future leaders from the US, Russia, China, India, and Brazil, paired with a German mentor.
eligibility: University graduates (bachelor's or higher) under 40, from one of the eligible countries, with a clear project proposal.
prepResources: A well-defined, feasible project proposal matters more than an ambitious but vague one — the Foundation evaluates project clarity closely.

title: KAUST Global Fellowship
org: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)
description: A fully funded PhD fellowship at KAUST in Saudi Arabia across water, energy, food, health, and digital research areas, with a substantial stipend and research budget.
eligibility: Prospective or current PhD students in a relevant STEM field, from any country.
prepResources: KAUST's research center pages list active faculty and projects — identifying a specific research fit strengthens the application.

title: Adam Smith Fellowship
org: Mercatus Center at George Mason University
